title: v1.158.0 cannot parse config files it writes — "yaml: line 2228: did not find expected key"
body:
## Summary

doctl `v1.158.0` (released 2026-05-07 16:21 UTC) cannot read its own config files. Every doctl invocation fails at startup with:

```
doctl: 2026/05/07 21:08:20 Config initialization failed: While parsing config: yaml: line 2228: did not find expected key
```

`v1.157.0` reads the **same file** successfully — verified.

## Reproduction

1. Install doctl v1.158.0
2. Run any doctl command (auth init, auth list, registry login, etc.)
3. The first invocation may write a default config (37,883 bytes / 2,227 lines on Linux x86_64); subsequent invocations fail to read it.

The error appears at line 2228 of a 2227-line file — i.e., doctl's parser expects more YAML content than the writer produced. The file ends mid-section with `vpcs.update.name: ""`.

## Evidence the file itself is valid YAML

`yaml.safe_load` (PyYAML) parses the file successfully:
- 91 top-level keys
- Well-formed structure (1-click, access-token, account, action, activations, agent, alert, api-url, apikeys, apps, ...)

So the failure is in doctl's parser, not the file.

## Evidence v1.157.0 reads the same file

```bash
$ wget https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/releases/download/v1.157.0/doctl-1.157.0-linux-amd64.tar.gz
$ tar xf doctl-1.157.0-linux-amd64.tar.gz
$ ./doctl --config=/home/USER/.config/doctl/config.yaml auth list
default (current)        # <-- success on the same file v1.158.0 fails on
```

## Suspected commit

The v1.157.0 → v1.158.0 changelog has only 2 commits:

- [#1835 DOKS SSO: per-cluster config, kubeconfig type](https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/pull/1835) — explicitly modifies config schema structure
- [#1839 Bump godo to v1.189.0](https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/pull/1839) — internal HTTP client only, unlikely cause

#1835 is the prime suspect — config schema additions can produce serialization output that the parser doesn't expect.

## Impact

- All CI runs using `digitalocean/action-doctl@v2` (which defaults to `version: 'latest'`) broke today within hours of the v1.158.0 release.
- Workaround: pin to `version: '1.157.0'` in workflow input.
- Long-term users with shared doctl config homes (e.g., self-hosted runners) are affected even after pinning, since the broken file persists until manually cleared. v1.157.0 reads the v1.158.0-written file fine, so the workaround does not require config cleanup.

## Environment

- doctl: v1.158.0
- OS: Ubuntu (self-hosted GitHub Actions runner, Linux x86_64)
- Action: `digitalocean/action-doctl@v2` with `version: 'latest'` (default)

## Workaround applied on our side

Pinning `version: '1.157.0'` in all `digitalocean/action-doctl@v2` callsites until v1.159.0 (or v1.158.x) ships a fix.

Happy to provide the corrupted config file (with secrets redacted) on request.

title: doctl apps list-job-invocations returns empty results when invocations exist
body:
## Bug Report

### doctl version
`doctl version 1.152.0-release`

### Description

`doctl apps list-job-invocations <app-id> <job-name>` returns an empty table with no results, even when the job has many invocations recorded by the platform.

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Create an App Platform app with a scheduled job (kind: SCHEDULED)
2. Wait for the job to be invoked (either by the scheduler or manually)
3. Run `doctl apps list-job-invocations <app-id> <job-name>`

### Expected Behavior

The command should list all job invocations with their ID, phase, timestamps, etc.

### Actual Behavior

The command returns an empty table:

```
$ doctl apps list-job-invocations <app-id> cron-test
ID    Job Name    Created At    Started At    Completed At    Phase
```

However, the underlying API returns data correctly:

```
$ curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "https://api.digitalocean.com/v2/apps/<app-id>/jobs/<job-name>/invocations" | python3 -m json.tool
```

Returns 13 invocations, all with `phase: SUCCEEDED`.

### Impact

This bug masked the real issue with our scheduled jobs — we spent hours thinking the cron scheduler was never firing, when in fact jobs were being invoked and failing. The empty CLI output made it appear that no invocations had ever occurred.

### Workaround

Use the API directly via `curl`.

### Environment
- OS: macOS (Darwin 24.2.0)
- doctl version: 1.152.0-release
- Shell: zsh

title: auth switch --context default writes bogus "default: true" into auth-contexts
body:
## Description

`doctl auth switch --context default` silently injects `default: "true"` into the `auth-contexts` map in `~/.config/doctl/config.yaml`. This entry is not a real token — it's a side effect of how `RunAuthSwitch` validates the requested context.

The bogus entry can break subsequent context switches because doctl may resolve the `default` context against `auth-contexts` (finding `"true"`) instead of the top-level `access-token`.

## Steps to reproduce

```bash
# Start with a clean config that has a named context
doctl auth init                              # sets top-level access-token
doctl auth init --context teamadf            # adds teamadf under auth-contexts

# Confirm no "default" key under auth-contexts
grep -A3 '^auth-contexts:' ~/.config/doctl/config.yaml

# Switch to default — this creates the bogus entry
doctl auth switch --context default

# Now check again
grep -A3 '^auth-contexts:' ~/.config/doctl/config.yaml
```

**Expected:**
```yaml
auth-contexts:
  teamadf: dop_v1_abc123...
```

**Actual:**
```yaml
auth-contexts:
  default: "true"
  teamadf: dop_v1_abc123...
```

The `default: "true"` entry appears every time you switch to the default context.

## Root cause

In [`commands/auth.go` → `RunAuthSwitch`](https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/blob/main/commands/auth.go), the default context is temporarily added to the auth-contexts map for validation:

```go
contextsAvail := viper.GetStringMap("auth-contexts")
contextsAvail[doctl.ArgDefaultContext] = true
```

Later, viper's state (now containing `default: true`) is serialized back to the config file via `writeConfig()`. The temporary validation entry is never removed before the write.

## Impact

- The `default` context resolves to the string `"true"` instead of the top-level `access-token`, which can cause API calls to silently use the wrong token or fail.
- Users managing multiple contexts (e.g. switching between teams) get stale results because all contexts fall back to the same top-level token.
- The entry reappears after every `doctl auth switch --context default`, so manually removing it doesn't stick.

## Suggested fix

Remove the `default` key from the map after validation, before writing the config. Something like:

```go
contextsAvail := viper.GetStringMap("auth-contexts")
contextsAvail[doctl.ArgDefaultContext] = true  // for validation only

// ... validation logic ...

// Clean up before persisting
delete(contextsAvail, doctl.ArgDefaultContext)
```

## Workaround

Strip the bogus entry after switching:

```bash
# Wrapper function
doswitch() {
  local ctx="${1:-default}"
  doctl auth switch --context "$ctx"
  sed -i '/^  default: "true"$/d' "${HOME}/.config/doctl/config.yaml" 2>/dev/null
}
```

## Environment

- doctl version: 1.110.0-release
- OS: Linux (WSL2)
- Shell: bash

title: `doctl registry` commands fail with 412 for multi-registry accounts without clear error or guidance
body:
### Description

All `doctl registry` subcommands (`repository list-v2`, `repository list-manifests`, `repository delete-manifest`, etc.) fail with an opaque 412 error when the account has multiple container registries, even though the `--registry` flag is documented as "optional."

This appears to be a server-side API change — these commands previously worked without `--registry`. There was no deprecation notice, changelog entry, or doctl release noting this breaking change.

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Have a DigitalOcean account with multiple container registries
2. Run any registry command without `--registry`:

```bash
doctl registry repository list-v2 --output json
```

### Expected Behavior

Either:
- A clear CLI-level error message like: `Error: multiple registries found. Please specify one with --registry <name>. Available: reg1, reg2`
- Or the command prompts the user to select a registry

### Actual Behavior

Raw API error passed through:

```json
{
  "errors": [
    {
      "detail": "failed to get registry: GET https://api.digitalocean.com/v2/registry: 412 (request \"...\") This API is not supported if you have created multiple registries. Please use '/v2/registries/{registry_name}' instead. Refer to https://docs.digitalocean.com/reference/api/digitalocean/#tag/Container-Registry for more info."
    }
  ]
}
```

### Suggested Fix

- Detect multi-registry accounts in doctl and surface a user-friendly error with available registry names
- Update the `--registry` flag description — it's not truly "optional" for multi-registry accounts
- Document this as a breaking change for users who previously had a single registry and later created a second one

### Environment

- `doctl` version: 1.151.0 (also reproduced with 1.141.0 via `digitalocean/action-doctl@v2`)
- Affected commands: `registry get`, `registry repository list-v2`, `registry repository list-manifests`, `registry repository delete-manifest`, `registry garbage-collection start`

### Impact

This silently broke CI/CD pipelines (GitHub Actions cron jobs) that were using `doctl registry` commands without `--registry`. The workaround is to add `--registry <name>` to every command (or positional arg for `garbage-collection start`).

title: registry repository list-v2 ignores --format and --no-header flags
body:
**Describe the Issue:**

The `--format` and `--no-header` flags are completely ignored by `doctl registry repository list-v2`. The command outputs all columns with headers regardless of the format specified.

**Steps to reproduce:**

```bash
# Expected: Only repository names, no header
doctl registry repository list-v2 --format Name --no-header

# Actual output: Full table with all columns and header
Name                       Latest Manifest                                                            Latest Tag    Tag Count    Manifest Count    Updated At
my-app-prod                sha256:a1b2c3d4e5f6a1b2c3d4e5f6a1b2c3d4e5f6a1b2c3d4e5f6a1b2c3d4e5f6a1b2    latest        2            3                 2025-12-04 06:57:34 +0000 UTC
my-app-stg                 sha256:f6e5d4c3b2a1f6e5d4c3b2a1f6e5d4c3b2a1f6e5d4c3b2a1f6e5d4c3b2a1f6e5    latest        3            5                 2025-12-11 18:35:05 +0000 UTC
```

**Expected behavior:**

```bash
doctl registry repository list-v2 --format Name --no-header
# Should output:
my-app-prod
my-app-stg
...
```

**Workaround:**

Using `--output json` with `jq` works correctly:
```bash
doctl registry repository list-v2 --output json | jq -r '.[].name'
```

**Environment:**
 - doctl version: 1.148.0 (also reproduced on 1.141.0 in CI)
 - OS: Ubuntu 22.04 / Ubuntu 24.04 (GitHub Actions)
 - Installation method: GitHub Actions `digitalocean/action-doctl@v2`

**Additional Details:**

The same issue affects `doctl registry repository list` (non-v2). This breaks scripts that rely on `grep -Fxq` for exact matching since the full row is returned instead of just the name.

Additional Note: We noticed this issue started to happen since December 27th

title: registries repository delete-tag returns success for images that don't exist
body:
**Describe the Issue:**

<!-- Please provide a clear and concise description of the problem you are
facing including the steps to reproduce the issue. -->

Hi there, I'm seeing that `doctl registries repository delete-tag` always returns `Successfully deleted 1 tag(s)`, so long as the requested registry exists.

I have made the following requests, all with the same output:

Request delete on repository that doesn't exist:
```
> doctl registries repository delete-tag my-registry not-a-repository my-tag
Successfully deleted 1 tag(s)
```

Request delete on tag that doesn't exist:
```
> doctl registries repository delete-tag my-registry my-repository not-a-tag
Successfully deleted 1 tag(s)
```

The CLI does error when I request delete on a registry that doesn't exist:
```
> doctl registries repository delete-tag not-a-registry my-repository my-tag
{"errors":[{"detail":"DELETE https://api.digitalocean.com/v2/registries/not-a-registry/repositories/my-repository/tags/my-tag: 404 (request \"requestId\") registry does not exist"}]}
```

Could the CLI be modified to more closely resemble the [API definition](https://docs.digitalocean.com/reference/api/digitalocean/#tag/Container-Registry/operation/registry_delete_repositoryTag)? For example, it would be great if when delete is requested on a tag that doesn't exist, the CLI returned 404.

As an aside, I notice there are `doctl registry` and `doctl registries` commands. Is there one that I should prefer over the other? For example, for `delete-tag`, I see these two versions:
- https://docs.digitalocean.com/reference/doctl/reference/registry/repository/delete-tag/
- https://docs.digitalocean.com/reference/doctl/reference/registries/repository/delete-tag/

Of note, the `doctl registry` versions of the same commands simply return nothing in all of the following cases:
- requesting delete on a tag that doesn't exist
- requesting delete on a repository that doesn't exist
- requesting delete on a tag that does exist
   - In this case, the tag is correctly deleted. But I don't get any feedback in the CLI that deletion was successful.

title: doctl databases pool create (fails when --user arg not provided)
body:
**Describe the Issue:**

1. According to the DO Dashboard, connection pools can have the user field (also known as `User privileges override (Optional)`) set to an empty string (which appears as `N/A` in the table shown on the Connection Pools tab for a Postgres database). The empty string corresponds to "Use inbound user" in the DO Dashboard. 

2. The [DO API Reference to "Add a New Connection Pool"](https://docs.digitalocean.com/reference/api/digitalocean/#tag/Databases/operation/databases_add_connectionPool) shows:
- `name`, `mode`, `size` and `db` are **required**, but 
- `user` is **not required**.

3. When using `doctl databases pool create <database-cluster-id> <pool-name> --size 25 --db <a-database-name-that-exists-inside-database-cluster-id-provided> --mode transaction`, I see an error message appear:
```
Error: (pool.create.user) command is missing required arguments
```
**See "Additional Details" below for a possible fix.**




**Environment:**
<!-- Please provide the following information: -->
 - doctl version: `1.142.0-release` (`Git commit hash: 4979b33d`)
 - OS: Ubuntu 20.04.4 LTS
 - Installation method: Installed binary from tarball (https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/releases/download/v1.142.0/doctl-1.142.0-linux-amd64.tar.gz)

**Additional Details:**

Since the `--user` arg is not marked as a `requiredOpt()` in https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/blob/c74c583764a12cc7ddb353acfe2733c5d20bc5d5/commands/databases.go#L1239-L1240
I believe the problem lies with 
https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/blob/c74c583764a12cc7ddb353acfe2733c5d20bc5d5/commands/databases.go#L1344-L1348
which probably needs to be modified so it doesn't throw an error if no `--user` arg is provided.

I can't post the `--trace` here, it's FAR too big. Is there any _other_ way to find the date a droplet was powered off via the CLI?

title: Improve `CONTRIBUTING.md` and `README.md` Files
body:
I would like to improve the `CONTRIBUTING.md` and `README.md` files by adding clear contribution guidelines, detailed setup instructions, and a project overview. Please assign this issue to me, Anchal Chaturvedi.


### Improving the `CONTRIBUTING.md` and `README.md` files is essential for several reasons:

1. **Enhanced Contributor Experience**: Clear guidelines and instructions help new contributors understand how to set up the project, adhere to coding standards, and follow the contribution process. This reduces the entry barrier for new contributors and encourages more people to contribute.

2. **Consistency and Quality**: Detailed contribution guidelines ensure that all contributions follow the same standards, maintaining the quality and consistency of the codebase. This is particularly important as the project grows and more contributors join.

3. **Better Understanding of the Project**: A comprehensive `README.md` file provides users and potential contributors with a clear understanding of the project's purpose, how to install and use it, and how they can contribute. This transparency fosters a more engaged and active community around the project.

### Examples:
- **Contribution Guidelines**: The `CONTRIBUTING.md` file can include sections like:
  - **Introduction**: Welcoming new contributors.
  - **Setup Instructions**: Step-by-step guide to setting up the development environment.
  - **Coding Standards**: Guidelines on code style and commit messages.
  - **Pull Request Process**: Steps to submit a pull request.

- **README.md**:
  - **Project Overview**: Briefly describe what the project does and its goals.
  - **Installation Instructions**: Commands and steps to install the project.
  - **Usage Instructions**: Examples of how to run and use the project.
  - **Contributing**: A link to the `CONTRIBUTING.md` file for detailed guidelines.

By implementing these changes, we can ensure that the project is more accessible, easier to contribute, and more attractive to new contributors. This ultimately leads to a more robust and well-maintained project.

title: Some inconsistencies in enum argument descriptions
body:
**Describe the Issue:**

I have done some more investigation related to this PR: https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/pull/1595, and I found more related issues / inconsistencies.

For instance in **doctl databases topics update**:

```
 --cleanup-policy string              Specifies the retention policy to use on log segments: Possible values are 'delete', 'compact_delete', 'compact' (default "delete")
 --compression-type string            Specifies the compression type for a kafka topic: Possible values are 'producer', 'gzip', 'snappy', 'Iz4', 'zstd', 'uncompressed' (default "producer")
```

but in **doctl databases create** and most other places:

```
--engine pg                          The database's engine. Possible values are: pg, `mysql`, `redis`, `mongodb`, `kafka` and `opensearch`. (default "pg")
```

In the first example, apostrophes don't interfere with command argument type generation and it stays defined as **string**.
In the second example, backticks are causing to use first value (**pg**) as data type for argument, which can be confusing.

I would be happy to create PR to fix this, however, I am not sure whether there's any reason attached to using backticks versus apostrophes. I am also not sure why default value is in quotes.

One possible solution is to introduce **enum** data type, and then argument descriptions could look like this:

```
--engine enum                          The database's engine. Possible enum values are: `pg`, `mysql`, `redis`, `mongodb`, `kafka` and `opensearch`. (default "pg")
```

title: Add --wait flag to databases resize command
body:
**What is the problem this feature would solve? Please describe.**
doctl aims to be useful in scripting. One way it does this is by providing a --wait flag for long running, async operations to block until they are complete rather than making the user implement polling in their own script. Some examples include:

- `doctl database create --wait`
- `doctl compute droplet create --wait`
- `doctl kubernetes cluster create --wait`
- `doctl apps create --wait`

Currently the `doctl databases resize` command does not have a `--wait` flag.

**Describe the solution you'd like**
Add a --wait flag to the `doctl databases resize` command. It should poll the [/v2/databases/$DATABASE_ID/resize](https://docs.digitalocean.com/reference/api/api-reference/#operation/databases_update_clusterSize), blocking until the database cluster's status attribute moves from resizing to online.

**Additional context**
Following the code path starting here demonstrates how `doctl database create`'s  `--wait` flag is implemented:

https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/blob/de1918ad1ee4b1e523c335fa9254e85d415d18aa/commands/apps.go#L282

title: Dependency inconsistency caused by `replace`
body:
**What is the problem this feature would solve? Please describe.**
Currently `doctl` depends on `github.com/digitalocean/godo v1.121.0` but do not follow to use the replace directive declared in `godo's` go.mod as below.  Repo `godo` uses replace directive to pin `github.com/stretchr/objx` to version `v0.2.0` and `golang.org/x/crypto` to version `v0.0.0-20200420201142-3c4aac89819a`.

```
replace github.com/stretchr/objx => github.com/stretchr/objx v0.2.0
replace golang.org/x/crypto => golang.org/x/crypto v0.0.0-20200420201142-3c4aac89819a
```

According to [Go Modules wikis](https://go.dev/doc/modules/gomod-ref#replace-notes), `replace` directives in modules other than the main module are ignored when building the main module. It means such replace usage in go.mod cannot be inherited by downstream projects automatically.  Consequently, `doctl's` version of `github.com/stretchr/objx` and `golang.org/x/crypto` is different from the pinned version `github.com/digitalocean/godo` needed.

**Possible Solution.**
1. Remove the replace usage in `github.com/digitalocean/godo`.
2. Add the same replace rule to your go.mod

title: Adding taints has wrong syntax in DO docs
body:
I have observed an issue in (probably) auto-generated docs under `doctl kubernetes cluster node-pool create` and `doctl kubernetes cluster node-pool update`.  Under Flags option `--taint`, the description says
> Taint in key[=value:]effect notation to apply to the node pool, repeat to add multiple taints at once. Omitted taints will be removed from the node pool if the flag is specified.

doctl kubernetes cluster node-pool update cluster-name node-pool-name --taint key=value:NoExecute

I am not sure whether the error is in the code (I was unable to locate that) or in auto-generation mechanism.

title: Ability to validate App Spec Schema in Offline Mode
body:
**What is the problem this feature would solve? Please describe.**

This feature would solve the following problems & issues:

- No ability to run the following command in "offline" mode. For example:

      doctl app spec validate .do/app.yml --schema-only
      Error: Unable to initialize DigitalOcean API client: access token is required. (hint: run 'doctl auth init')

  - digitalocean/action-doctl#67
  - LyraPhase/docker-phasik.tv#5
- Security Risk: GitHub Actions using [digitalocean/action-doctl][1] for App Schema validation always requires an API `token`
  - The current action & implementation for `doctl app schema validate --schema-only ...` makes it impossible to follow the [principle of least priviledge][2]
  - This prevents [a proposed `no_auth` feature for digitalocean/action-doctl][3] from being currently possible.

[1]: https://github.com/digitalocean/action-doctl
[2]: https://securitylab.github.com/research/github-actions-building-blocks/#following-the-principle-of-least-privilege
[3]: https://github.com/trinitronx/action-doctl/pull/1

**Describe the solution you'd like**

Some way to validate a DigitalOcean App Spec in offline mode should be provided.  A separate command (e.g. `validate-offline`) should be decoupled from the `godo` API client & `auth` initialization to support this use case.

**Additional context**

Currently, the `doctl app spec validate` command is tightly coupled to require `godo` init and `doctl auth`.  The `validateCommand` is defined using [`CmdBuilder()`][4], which calls [`cmdBuilderWithInit(..., true, options...)`][5] with [`initCmd`][6] set to [`true` **always**][7] (This is passed to [`NewCmdConfig()`][8] as the [`initGodo` parameter which runs `cmdConfig.initServices()`][9]).

As such, the `--schema-only` flag still requires `doctl auth` and a `godo` client is initialized.  Unfortunately due to the way Cobra commands appear to be set up, the `--schema-only` flag is not available in the context where the command creation happens.  Therefore, it seems that we must implement a new sub-command for this feature (e.g. `doctl app spec validate-offline`). The usage of [`cmdBuilderWithInit` similar to this command PR][10] seems to be the proper method to setup this new command.

[4]: https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/blob/f6d249bcc18b0712bc8c3048c76addea25ec6b7d/commands/command.go#L66-L68
[5]: https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/blob/f6d249bcc18b0712bc8c3048c76addea25ec6b7d/commands/command.go#L70
[6]: https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/blob/f6d249bcc18b0712bc8c3048c76addea25ec6b7d/commands/command.go#L81C5-L81C12
[7]: https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/blob/f6d249bcc18b0712bc8c3048c76addea25ec6b7d/commands/command.go#L67C75-L67C79
[8]: https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/blob/f6d249bcc18b0712bc8c3048c76addea25ec6b7d/commands/command_config.go#L78
[9]: https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/blob/f6d249bcc18b0712bc8c3048c76addea25ec6b7d/commands/command_config.go#L193-L197
[10]: https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/pull/225

title: 403 response on cluster kubeconfig save from the pipeline in Gitlab
body:
We've been using Gitlab CI since forever and everything worked fine until this night. Started from this morning the `doctl kubernetes cluster kubeconfig save` command fails from the pipeline resultin in a 403 response.

The exact same commands do work when executed locally.

title: Not Possible to See CName Domain Record Tags from the CLI with Doctl?
body:
**What is the problem this feature would solve? Please describe.**

The command `doctl compute domain records list somedomain.com` omits the "tag" column (used exclusively for CName domain records), although this information becomes available by adding on `--trace` (making the tag type visible via JSON).

**Describe the solution you'd like**

Show the tag-column. Optional: Make an argument `--tag-column`?

**Additional context**

EXAMPLE:

This command and its output:

```
root@lamp-s-1vcpu-1gb-nyc1-01:~# doctl compute domain records list sortwords.com
ID            Type    Name             Data                              Priority    Port    TTL     Weight
1706491642    SOA     @                1800                              0           0       1800    0
1706491643    NS      @                ns1.===              0           0       1800    0
1706491644    NS      @                ns2.===              0           0       1800    0
1706491645    NS      @                ns3.===              0           0       1800    0
1706492491    AAAA    *                2604===       0           0       3600    0
1706492492    AAAA    @                2604===       0           0       3600    0
1706492608    A       *                198.===                   0           0       3600    0
1706492609    A       @                198.===                   0           0       3600    0
1706564261    CAA     sortwords.com    mailto:===    0           0       3600    0

title: doctl auth init -t TOKEN should not print token to stdout
body:
**Replication Steps**

When using doctl the default behavior of token auth, is to print the token to stdout per `<redacted>`.
```
doctl auth init -t $TOKEN

Using token [<redacted>]

Validating token... OK
```

This behavior is undesirable as the default behavior because security. 

**Desired Behavior**
No tokens or sensitive information are sent to stdout.

**Actual Behavior**
A sensitive token is printed to stdout which can be inadvertedly leaked.

**Reasoning**
Security. When doctl is used in CI (or other scenarios), it results in tokens being written to stdout which is often stored in a log state, creating opportunity for tokens to be leaked, unccessarily.

It is a requirement to authenticate docker with the DO registry via doctl, so it's a critical use case.

If this DX intended to help users verify their token, maybe default off and flag on with `--verify`?

Thanks for the great work on the CLI. Happy to offer any further feedback that will help 🤘

title: Allow `--build-command` to be passed for docker builds
body:
**What is the problem this feature would solve? Please describe.**
<!-- A clear and concise description of what the problem is. Ex. I'm always frustrated when [...] -->

We're trying to improve the performance of our Docker based builds for DOAP, and was looking into Depot (https://depot.dev), which is a drop-in replacement for Docker. So `docker build` -> `depot build`.

Unfortunately the `doctl app dev build` command doesn't support a custom build command for docker builds. 

```
✘ build command overrides are ignored for dockerfile-based builds
```

**Describe the solution you'd like**
<!-- A clear and concise description of what you want to happen. -->

It would be nice if we could substitute `docker` for `depot`. Whether it's something like `--build-command 'depot build'` or a completely unique option like `--use-depot`.

**Additional context**
<!-- Add any other context about the feature request here. -->

title: Add --wait flag to load-balancer create command
body:
**What is the problem this feature would solve? Please describe.**

`doctl` aims to be useful in scripting. One way it does this is by providing a `--wait` flag for long running, async operations to block until until they are complete rather than making the user implement polling in their own script. Some examples include:

- `doctl compute droplet create --wait`
- `doctl kubernetes cluster create --wait`
- `doctl apps create --wait`

Currently the `doctl compute load-balancer create` command does not have a `--wait` flag.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

Add a `--wait` flag to the `doctl compute load-balancer create` command. It should poll the [`/v2/load_balancers/$LOAD_BALANCER_ID` endpoint](https://docs.digitalocean.com/reference/api/api-reference/#operation/loadBalancers_get), blocking until the load balancer's `status` attribute moves from `new` to `active`.

**Additional context**

Following the code path starting here demonstrates how `doctl apps create`'s  `--wait` flag is implemented:

https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/blob/de1918ad1ee4b1e523c335fa9254e85d415d18aa/commands/apps.go#L282

title: Add --wait flag to database create command
body:
**What is the problem this feature would solve? Please describe.**

`doctl` aims to be useful in scripting. One way it does this is by providing a `--wait` flag for long running, async operations to block until until they are complete rather than making the user implement polling in their own script. Some examples include:

- `doctl compute droplet create --wait`
- `doctl kubernetes cluster create --wait`
- `doctl apps create --wait`

Currently the `doctl database create` command does not have a `--wait` flag.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

Add a `--wait` flag to the `doctl database create` command. It should poll the [`/v2/databases/$DATABASE_ID` endpoint](https://docs.digitalocean.com/reference/api/api-reference/#operation/databases_get_cluster), blocking until the database cluster's `status` attribute moves from `creating` to `online`.

**Additional context**

Following the code path starting here demonstrates how `doctl apps create`'s  `--wait` flag is implemented:

https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/blob/de1918ad1ee4b1e523c335fa9254e85d415d18aa/commands/apps.go#L282

One important difference for databases is that some database engines (mongodb now, but potentially others) only return the password in the initial response to the create request. It is _not_ included in subsequent GET responses. So that value must be retained.

title: [Feature Request] Deploy zip to Functions
body:
**What is the problem this feature would solve? Please describe.**
<!-- A clear and concise description of what the problem is. Ex. I'm always frustrated when [...] -->

Using this folder structure:

- project.yml
- packages
  - sample
    - example
      - index.js
      - package.json
      - node_modules

To deploy to DigitalOcean Functions, I need to run `doctl serverless deploy .`.
Then it will run `npm install --production` and then it will zip the content and deploy to Functions.

**Describe the solution you'd like**
<!-- A clear and concise description of what you want to happen. -->

I have a library to zip the contents of `node_modules` and minify the files called [node_modules_packer](https://github.com/H4ad/node-modules-packer) and I want to use this library to create a zip file and then upload it to Functions.

Serverless Framework has a solution called [artifact](https://www.serverless.com/framework/docs/providers/aws/guide/packaging#artifact), which looks like:

```yml
package:
  artifact: path/to/my-artifact.zip
```

packages:
  - name: sample
    functions:
      - name: hello
        file: 'path/to/my-artifact.zip'
        zipped: true
        main: ''
        runtime: 'nodejs:18'
        web: true
```

**Additional context**
<!-- Add any other context about the feature request here. -->

I spent a couple hours reading the source code of this CLI my discovers was: `doctl` call [@nimbella/nimbella-cli](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@nimbella/nimbella-cli), and then call [@nimbella/nimbella-deployer](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@nimbella/nimbella-deployer).

The `@nimbella/nimbella-deployer` has support to deploy the content of the Function as zip file [here](https://github.com/nimbella/nimbella-deployer/blob/4841ca48f49ea1ce9244906294a6e35298469c75/src/deploy-struct.ts#L62) and [here](https://github.com/nimbella/nimbella-deployer/blob/4841ca48f49ea1ce9244906294a6e35298469c75/src/deploy-struct.ts#L57).

Vulnerability Details

runc is a CLI tool for spawning and running containers on Linux according to the OCI specification. A bug was found in runc prior to version 1.1.2 where `runc exec --cap` created processes with non-empty inheritable Linux process capabilities, creating an atypical Linux environment and enabling programs with inheritable file capabilities to elevate those capabilities to the permitted set during execve(2). This bug did not affect the container security sandbox as the inheritable set never contained more capabilities than were included in the container's bounding set. This bug has been fixed in runc 1.1.2. This fix changes `runc exec --cap` behavior such that the additional capabilities granted to the process being executed (as specified via `--cap` arguments) do not include inheritable capabilities. In addition, `runc spec` is changed to not set any inheritable capabilities in the created example OCI spec (`config.json`) file.

title: `apps update --wait` may hang forever when the deployment is a no-op
body:
Hi,

I'm trying to use `doctl apps update --wait` in a continuous deployment pipeline. Sometimes this works fine, other times the command hangs until max attempts. Looking at the source, I think I can see the problem.

https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/blob/62602dba745d478962fa029f2d2a2c61bb4f93e0/commands/apps.go#L352-L355

This is where the `wait` flag is applied. `waitForActiveDeployment` is called with an empty string as the deployment ID.

https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/blob/62602dba745d478962fa029f2d2a2c61bb4f93e0/commands/apps.go#L452-L460

Inside `waitForActiveDeployment`, if the deployment ID is empty the app is fetched and `deploymentID` is set from the `InProgressDeployment` **if it's non-nil**.

However, what I can see if I hit the `update` command a few times is that sometimes `in_progress_deployment` isn't set right after updating, presumably when there are no changes to make. When this is the case, `update --wait` will loop for `maxAttempts` since `deploymentID` will remain `""` and `app.InProgressDeployment.ID` will remain `nil`.

I'm not too sure of the semantics of the DO API, but the response doesn't seem to include anything that could strictly correlate to the update. If the `update` response could be assumed to contain the `InProgressDeployment` if it's not a no-op, then it might work to only call `waitForActiveDeployment` if `app.InProgressDeployment != nil`, and pass long the `DeploymentID` otherwise.

Vulnerability Details

client_golang is the instrumentation library for Go applications in Prometheus, and the promhttp package in client_golang provides tooling around HTTP servers and clients. In client_golang prior to version 1.11.1, HTTP server is susceptible to a Denial of Service through unbounded cardinality, and potential memory exhaustion, when handling requests with non-standard HTTP methods. In order to be affected, an instrumented software must use any of `promhttp.InstrumentHandler*` middleware except `RequestsInFlight`; not filter any specific methods (e.g GET) before middleware; pass metric with `method` label name to our middleware; and not have any firewall/LB/proxy that filters away requests with unknown `method`. client_golang version 1.11.1 contains a patch for this issue. Several workarounds are available, including removing the `method` label name from counter/gauge used in the InstrumentHandler; turning off affected promhttp handlers; adding custom middleware before promhttp handler that will sanitize the request method given by Go http.Request; and using a reverse proxy or web application firewall, configured to only allow a limited set of methods.

title: CVE-2021-43784 (Medium) detected in github.com/opencontainers/runc-v1.0.0-rc95
body:
## CVE-2021-43784 - Medium Severity Vulnerability
Vulnerable Library - github.com/opencontainers/runc-v1.0.0-rc95

CLI tool for spawning and running containers according to the OCI specification

Dependency Hierarchy: - github.com/docker/cli-v19.03.13-beta1 (Root Library) - github.com/docker/docker-v20.10.13 - :x: **github.com/opencontainers/runc-v1.0.0-rc95** (Vulnerable Library)

Found in base branch: main

Vulnerability Details

runc is a CLI tool for spawning and running containers on Linux according to the OCI specification. In runc, netlink is used internally as a serialization system for specifying the relevant container configuration to the `C` portion of the code (responsible for the based namespace setup of containers). In all versions of runc prior to 1.0.3, the encoder did not handle the possibility of an integer overflow in the 16-bit length field for the byte array attribute type, meaning that a large enough malicious byte array attribute could result in the length overflowing and the attribute contents being parsed as netlink messages for container configuration. This vulnerability requires the attacker to have some control over the configuration of the container and would allow the attacker to bypass the namespace restrictions of the container by simply adding their own netlink payload which disables all namespaces. The main users impacted are those who allow untrusted images with untrusted configurations to run on their machines (such as with shared cloud infrastructure). runc version 1.0.3 contains a fix for this bug. As a workaround, one may try disallowing untrusted namespace paths from your container. It should be noted that untrusted namespace paths would allow the attacker to disable namespace protections entirely even in the absence of this bug.

**Describe the Issue:**

When running the command `doctl kubernetes cluster kubeconfig save {cluster-name OR cluster-id}` the context name, cluster name and auth user name are not unique across multiple Digital Ocean accounts or Teams.  This causes authentication to be merged over multiple accounts.

For example if I create the same kubernetes cluster in two teams (e.g. demo-cluster) in the same region (e.g. lon1) the same context, cluster and auth name will be created for both clusters, `do-lon1-demo-cluster`.

Maybe adding an additional flag to allow an override to the context name that could also be used for making the cluster and auth names unique may fix the issue?

title: CVE-2021-41190 (Medium) detected in github.com/opencontainers/image-spec-v1.0.1
body:
## CVE-2021-41190 - Medium Severity Vulnerability
Vulnerable Library - github.com/opencontainers/image-spec-v1.0.1

OCI Image Format

Dependency Hierarchy: - github.com/docker/cli-v20.10.6 (Root Library) - github.com/docker/docker-v17.03.2-ce - :x: **github.com/opencontainers/image-spec-v1.0.1** (Vulnerable Library)

Found in HEAD commit: 1aa36a8304905676b86be6b9038b872193d94378

Found in base branch: main

Vulnerability Details

The OCI Distribution Spec project defines an API protocol to facilitate and standardize the distribution of content. In the OCI Distribution Specification version 1.0.0 and prior, the Content-Type header alone was used to determine the type of document during push and pull operations. Documents that contain both “manifests” and “layers” fields could be interpreted as either a manifest or an index in the absence of an accompanying Content-Type header. If a Content-Type header changed between two pulls of the same digest, a client may interpret the resulting content differently. The OCI Distribution Specification has been updated to require that a mediaType value present in a manifest or index match the Content-Type header used during the push and pull operations. Clients pulling from a registry may distrust the Content-Type header and reject an ambiguous document that contains both “manifests” and “layers” fields or “manifests” and “config” fields if they are unable to update to version 1.0.1 of the spec.

      - --context=default
      - <id>
      command: goland
      env: null
      interactiveMode: IfAvailable
      provideClusterInfo: false
```

I haven't looked at the source, but I imagine it's using the executable name from `os.Args`, which is likely being modified by the goland terminal. It must be passing all commands through `goland` first. 

I assume the reason for doing this is so that someone can rename the `doctl` executable, but perhaps that should just be hard-coded instead.

The API documentation for [update_domain_record](https://docs.digitalocean.com/reference/api/api-reference/#operation/update_domain_record) does not indicate that the field is nullable :-( but a test with a value of 0 shows that the existing TTL is preserved.

169333525    A       foo     192.168.1.2    0           0       600    0

title: Requesting a `databases firewalls remove --rule` flag
body:
**What is the problem this feature would solve? Please describe.**
For an application I'm building I would like to have a GitHub action temporarily connect to a database to perform some migrations. The machine that the action runs on is going to be one of many different machines with an IP address that's hard to know ahead of time. I would like to add the ip address rule tag temporarily, execute the migration, then remove it.

Adding the rule is straightforward and can be done with the following github action YAML:

```yaml
  - name: Determine Public IP
    id: ip
    uses: haythem/public-ip@v1.2

  - name: Allow GitHub CI Runner
    run: doctl databases firewalls append ${{ secrets.DO_FW_ID }} --rule ip_addr:${{ steps.ip.outputs.ipv4 }}
```

The output of this command looks something like this:

```
UUID      ClusterUUID  Type     Value
UUID_1    DB_ID_1    ip_addr    1.2.3.4
UUID_1    DB_ID_1    ip_addr    AA.BB.CC.DD
UUID_1    DB_ID_2    ip_addr    10.XX.XX.XX/XX
```

**Describe the solution you'd like**
Unfortunately, scripting a solution to then remove the rule is quite a bit more complex. One needs to grep the output from the previous command searching for an occurrence of the IP that was provided as input, sed/awk out the first column, assign that value somehow to another variable, and use it later for the removal command.

What would be convenient is if the `remove` subcommand was symmetrical to the `append` subcommand. For example, the following would be straightforward to implement (and also be more intuitive--it's the first thing I reached for):

```yaml
  - name: Disallow GitHub CI Runner
    run: doctl databases firewalls remove ${{ secrets.DO_FW_ID }} --rule ip_addr:${{ steps.ip.outputs.ipv4 }}
```

**Additional context**
I believe the functionality was added in #950. In that PR the author even hinted that the `remove` command would follow the `doctl databases firewalls remove --rule type:value` pattern.

I do see that it's possible to have duplicate tags (like `ip_addr:1.2.3.4` repeated twice), which is perhaps the reason why a UUID is used for removal instead of the tag. That said, I believe adding the `remove --rule` flag and have it remove all repeated rules would be the desired behavior. This is kind of like comparing the linux `kill PID` command (analogous to the `--uuid` flag) with the `killall COMMAND` command (analogous to the proposed `--rule` flag), where different scenarios requires one or the other.

title: "doctl auth switch" mangles all auth-contexts keys that are email addresses
body:
### Describe the Issue:

With one or more auth contexts that are email addresses (eg `test@example.com`), using `doctl auth switch` mangles ALL `auth-contexts` keys in the config file: it removes the TLD substring (eg `com`) from each original email address key, and makes it a "sub key" under the now-mangled email address key:

A `~/.config/doctl/config.yaml` with working contexts/API keys:
```
auth-contexts:
  test@example.com: my_secret_api_key
  test2@example.org: another_secret_api_key
```

The same `~/.config/doctl/config.yaml`, after a `doctl auth switch`:
```
auth-contexts:
  test@example:
    com: my_secret_api_key
  test2@example:
    org: another_secret_api_key
```
----
### Steps to reproduce:

1. Just showing I am doing this from scratch:
```
$ rm -rf ~/.config/doctl/config.yaml

$ doctl auth list
default (current)
```

2. Run a `doctl auth init` and verify the new context:
```
$ doctl auth init --context "test@example.com"
Please authenticate doctl for use with your DigitalOcean account. You can generate a token in the control panel at https://cloud.digitalocean.com/account/api/tokens

Enter your access token:
Validating token... OK

$ doctl auth list
test@example.com
default (current)
```

3. Run a `doctl auth switch` (with or without arguments; this will mangle ALL auth contexts that are email addresses)
```
$ doctl auth switch
Now using context [default] by default

$ doctl auth list
test@example
default (current)
```

----

I assume this is happening [somewhere around here](https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/blob/00cddfcca309bdb8c5fb46b5399218ceae39a28b/commands/auth.go#L234), so not 100% sure it's a `doctl` thing, or upstream `gopkg.in/yaml.v2` / `spf13/viper` issues.

----

**Environment:**
<!-- Please provide the following information: -->
 - doctl version: 1.61.0-release
 - OS: Fedora 33
 - Installation method: GitHub release for Linux

**Additional Details:**

title: Add ability to switch context using environment variable
body:
**What is the problem this feature would solve? Please describe.**
<!-- A clear and concise description of what the problem is. Ex. I'm always frustrated when [...] -->

It would be really nice to have an ability to change `doctl` configuration for the current session with environment variables, similar to `aws-cli`.

This problem is the most apparent when the user needs to switch between multiple contexts.

**Describe the solution you'd like**
<!-- A clear and concise description of what you want to happen. -->

Adding an ability to define an environment variable `DIGITALOCEAN_CONTEXT` as an alternative way to switch context for the current session only:

```bash
$ doctl auth list
default (current)
my-context
$ DIGITALOCEAN_CONTEXT=my-context doctl auth list
default
my-context (current)

This will be more in line with `aws-cli` which uses `AWS_DEFAULT_PROFILE` environment variable for the same purpose.

title: Fix the configuration removing command
body:
**Describe the Issue:**

```

rm -rf "$HOME/Library/Application Support/doctl"

title: Add wait option to app create and update commands
body:
**What is the problem this feature would solve? Please describe.**

In CI and/or scripting it is often useful for commands that create resources to block until they become ready. For example, both `doctl compute droplet create` and `doctl kubernetes cluster` create have `--wait` flags that block until the resource has been successfully created. Recently, the `doctl apps create-deployment` command has grown a `--wait` flag as well.

Creating or updating an App can also start a new deployment and should have the same behavior. 

**Describe the solution you'd like**

`doctl apps create` and  `doctl apps update` should accept a  `--wait` flag that will block until any new deployment they trigger reaches the `ACTIVE` phase.

**Additional context**

This is a continuation of the theme from https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/issues/964 (PR: https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/pull/969).

**Describe the Issue:**

title: Support for getting do_token from Vault
body:
**What is the problem this feature would solve? Please describe.**
Now, `doctl` after auth init action, saves long-lived token for DO resources in a file system (config file).

**Describe the solution you'd like**
`doctl auth` should allow bypass the local storage and get the desired token from Vault repository. In that case, short-lived Vaults tokens will allow to getting long-lived. Similar features are available in terms of shadowing secrets on well-known cloud providers as with the AWS, Azure etc.
 
So the desired scenario might look like:
1. Call `doctl auth init` for getting Vault connection properties.
2. Call `vault login` in order to login into Vault (e.g. via OIDC provider, GitHub etc.)
3. Use `doctl` as usual, but in the background, `doctl` uses Vault client and honoured the default token from `~/.vault-token` in terms of getting target DO token.

**Additional context**
This suggestion improves significantly security in case of using `doctl` on the desktop env. It avoids storing DO tokens locally on fs.

title: How to update DNS records? Receiving "Data needs to end with a dot (.)"
body:
**Describe the Issue:**

See: https://www.digitalocean.com/community/questions/how-to-update-dns-records-receiving-data-needs-to-end-with-a-dot

The issue is in the API but is surfaced through `doctl` too:

```bash
doctl compute domain records update ${DOMAIN} \
--record-id ${ID} \
--record-ttl 43200

Error: PUT https://api.digitalocean.com/v2/domains/.../records/...: 422 Data needs to end with a dot (.)
```

The workaround works when applied to `doctl` too (adding `--record-data`):

```bash
doctl compute domain records update ${DOMAIN} \
--record-id ${ID} \
--record-ttl 43200 \
--record-data "${DATA}."
```

**Describe the Issue:**

<!-- Please provide a clear and concise description of the problem you are
facing including the steps to reproduce the issue. -->

We trying to use the zsh completion generated by doctl with a sed version not supporting the `_filedir` extension (so busybox sed, that is used by default on alpine linux)


**Environment:**
<!-- Please provide the following information: -->
 - doctl version:
both the last release:
```doctl version 1.52.0-release
Git commit hash: af5bae4
```
and the one packaged on alpine:
```
doctl version 0.0.0-dev
```
 - OS: alpine linux edge and 3.1.12
 - Installation method: using the [distribution package](https://pkgs.alpinelinux.org/package/edge/testing/x86_64/doctl) and downloading from [the release page](https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/releases/tag/v1.52.0).

**Additional Details:**
Here is a detailed how to to reproduce the bug. It assumes you have docker installed and running, and your containers can access internet.

### Running a clean alpine container with docker:
`docker run -it alpine /bin/sh`

### Setup in the container
```shell
# apk add zsh
# zsh
# autoload -Uz compinit
# compinit
# wget https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/releases/download/v1.52.0/doctl-1.52.0-linux-amd64.tar.gz
# tar -C /usr/local/bin -zxvf doctl-1.52.0-linux-amd64.tar.gz
```

The bug in itself
```shell
# source <(doctl completion zsh)
```

You should get the following output
```
sed: bad regex '[[:<:]]_filedir[[:>:]]': Unknown character class name
```

Note: If we used the alpine provided [doctl-zsh-completion](https://pkgs.alpinelinux.org/package/edge/testing/x86_64/doctl-zsh-completion) package, the same bug would appear when trying to use the autocomplete (ie, `doctl [tab]`)


It seems the bug comes from [line 600 of the completion.go file](https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/blob/5d55b2d49a8bc47043d64c5ccd6f879c6cf10535/commands/completion.go#L600).

title: `doctl compute droplet get <droplet-id|droplet-name>`
body:
**What is the problem this feature would solve? Please describe.**

`doctl compute droplet get` supports only droplet ID whereas `doctl compute droplet delete` supports ID and name

```console
doctl compute droplet get --help

Usage:
  doctl compute droplet get <droplet-id> [flags]

doctl compute droplet delete --help

Usage:
  doctl compute droplet delete <droplet-id|droplet-name>... [flags]
```

**Describe the solution you'd like**

It would be helpful to `doctl compute droplet get <droplet-name>` to save `doctl compute droplet list` and then either `grep`ping the ID  or `--output=JSON` and then `jq`ing it, in order to get values from a specific droplet's configuration.

Or, I'm missing something!?

title: SSH key public_key not accessible via doctl
body:
**What is the problem this feature would solve? Please describe.**
When `list`ing or `get`ting a ssh-key, with the `doctl compute ssh-key` subcommand, it's possible to see the fingerprint of the key, but not possible to see the actual public key, which would be useful

**Describe the solution you'd like**
an optional format for the ssh-key field, e.g. `--format PublicKey` that would show the public key string `ssh-rsa ...`. The string is usually very long so showing it should be optional

**Additional context**
The value of that string is already in the API request, at the `public_key` attribute

 - Installation method: snap

**Additional Details:**
The first time I ran the command, I got an error about permissions:
```
~$ doctl registry login
Logging Docker in to registry.digitalocean.com
WARNING: Error loading config file: /home/coo/.docker/config.json: open /home/coo/.docker/config.json: permission denied
Warning: Using the doctl Snap? Grant access to the doctl:dot-docker plug to use this command with: sudo snap connect doctl:dot-docker
Error: open /home/coo/.docker/config.json761575089: permission denied
```

I executed said command `sudo snap connect doctl:dot-docker` and afterwards I got the error above.

Note that the secret service binary does exist and is available in `/usr/bin`
```
~$ which docker-credential-secretservice
/usr/bin/docker-credential-secretservice
```

I'm almost certain that this is something to do with the isolation of snap applications. If I had to guess, I'd say the environment the snap runs in doesn't have the path variables, or the access to `/usr/bin`. But that's a, perhaps unhelpful, guess.

title: Several misspellings in doctl
body:
### Overview

There are several misspellings in our codebase. These can be found with the Go tool [misspell](https://github.com/client9/misspell).

### How to fix it

1. Clone this repo.
2. If you're using the `misspell` tool, you'll notice that there are quite a few issues reported. Most of these are in vendored code, so to make it easier, these are the only ones affecting this actual codebase:
    1. `commands/account.go:27:65`: "retreive" is a misspelling of "retrieve"
    2. `commands/certificates.go:85:36`: "comfirmation" is a misspelling of "confirmation"
    3. `commands/image_actions.go:48:123`: "inlcuding" is a misspelling of "including"
    4. `commands/completion.go:165:35`: "commmand" is a misspelling of "command"
    5. `integration/cdn_create_test.go:84:11`: "orgin" is a misspelling of "origin"
    6. `integration/cdn_create_test.go:100:46`: "orgin" is a misspelling of "origin"
3. Once those misspellings have been fixed, open a pull request to this repo with your changes. If you want, mention this issue in your pull request by writing something like, "Closes #870" which will close this issue upon being merged.

title: Top-level doctl package fails golint check
Apps integration test fails to check a returned error
body:
### Overview

Our [Go Report Card](https://goreportcard.com/report/github.com/digitalocean/doctl) shows that we're failing to get a perfect score on the `ineffassign` check due to a [single error](https://goreportcard.com/report/github.com/digitalocean/doctl#ineffassign). That error is in our integration testing suite, and it's that our apps integration test fails to check a returned error.

### How to fix it

1. Clone this repo.
2. If you haven't already, install Go through their [official installer](https://golang.org/dl/).
3. In [`doctl/integration/apps_spec_test.go:145`](https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/blob/master/integration/apps_spec_test.go#L145), we're failing to check the `err` returned from the `json.Marshal(testAppSpec)` call. Just below that line, you'll need to add an `expect.NoError(err)` statement.
4. Open a pull request to this repo with your changes. If you want, mention this issue in your pull request by writing something like, "Closes #867" which will close this issue upon being merged.

title: Run gofmt on doctl's codebase
body:
### Overview

Right now, [Go Report Card](https://goreportcard.com/report/github.com/digitalocean/doctl) reports that we have so gofmt failures in our codebase.

Typically, these can fixed by running `gofmt -s -w ./` from the root of the project.

### How to fix it

1. Clone this repo.
2. If you haven't already, install Go through their [official installer](https://golang.org/dl/).
3. From the root of this project, run `gofmt -s -w ./`. The `-s` flag is short for "simplify", and will make some changes to make the code simpler. The `-w` flag is short for "write", which means that the changes will be written to disk instead of shown in stdout.
4. Open a pull request to this repo with your changes. If you want, mention this issue in your pull request by writing something like, "Closes #866" which will close this issue upon being merged.

title: Allow picking the context’s name in `doctl kubernetes cluster kubeconfig save`
body:
**What is the problem this feature would solve? Please describe.**
Currently, `doctl kubernetes cluster kubeconfig save <NAME>` saves a `kubectl` context named `do-<REGION>-<NAME>`. This makes it difficult to use `doctl` in continuous integration runs, because the name it configures `kubectl` with depends on also knowing the region of the cluster.

**Describe the solution you'd like**
Adding an `--alias=name` option (I picked that name from `aws eks update-kubeconfig`), which saves authentication information under the specified context, would allow control over the context’s name. By making the name predictable, we can then use it elsewhere in CI pipelines, or other scripts locally.
